Collocating is often not feasible. Meetings only with team members in your area excludes distributed members. Setting up separate area leaders creates silos and reduces collaboration. Technology-enabled transparency and ongoing contact keeps all team members engaged and informed.
topic: Distributed Project Teams, Remote Team Management, Communication

question: Your team has been working at a sustainable pace, but your organization is moving to an agile way of work. The team has hesitations about this transition. What should you do?
options: A. Add extra resources to your team to deliver faster and avoid executives getting involved
B. Proceed with a predictive development approach and deal with the consequences later
C. Ask your team to work with agile velocity and iterations despite their hesitations
D. Suggest the team uses flow-based scheduling with a Kanban board for visual work management
explanation: Flow-based scheduling with Kanban boards is a proven agile approach that respects team capacity and maintains focus through work-in-progress limits, minimizing context switching and multitasking. This addresses the transition to agile while supporting sustainable pace. Adding resources may complicate things, forcing the team against their will reduces engagement, and avoiding the organizational mandate is not viable. Kanban is one of the major agile approaches alongside Scrum and Lean, optimizing deliveries based on resource capacity and minimizing waste.

question: Your team has never worked on projects before and doesn't know about waterfall, agile, or different development models. Your project may have requirements that are certain or uncertain with varying levels of risk, and you are unsure which approach to use as you begin this new project. What will you do next?
options: A. Use waterfall and plan everything up front so the team has more stability
B. Use general phase definitions such as feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close
C. Use agile to ensure you can respond to change while your team's capability increases
D. Don't use any project approach; it doesn't matter as long as the work is getting done
explanation: All projects—whether waterfall or agile—go through the same fundamental phases: feasibility, design, build, test, deploy, and close. In waterfall these are done sequentially once; in agile they are done multiple times for each feature or increment. This approach provides a universally applicable starting point regardless of uncertainty or team experience. It allows flexibility in how these phases are implemented while ensuring a structured framework is in place.
topic: Project Life Cycles and Phase Definitions

question: You have noted nine epics for delivery as part of your most recent project. Your team have broken down these epics into user stories and would like to see the effort involved for these deliverables. You ask your team to find the smallest user story, size it with a one, and then determine the size of the other cards based on how they compare to the original card. What approach are you taking?
options: A. Relative estimating
B. Deterministic estimating
C. Absolute estimating
D. Probabilistic estimating
explanation: This approach describes relative estimating, where you establish a baseline (the smallest user story sized as one) and then estimate all other items in comparison to that baseline. This is commonly used in agile environments and forms the basis for story point estimation. Deterministic and absolute estimating produce specific fixed numbers. Probabilistic estimating involves probability ranges and confidence levels, not comparative sizing.

question: You are working on a project that has recently moved to a more agile way of work. The team have not completely taken on the scrum approach however and are comfortable working with a more flow-based approach, moving user stories across a kanban board. You are wanting to get an estimate on how much work can be completed in the next three months based on the team's existing process progress. What will you do next?
options: A. Multiply the velocity by the number of remaining user stories
B. Multiply the cycle time by the throughput
C. Use story points to forecast delivery
D. Calculate sprint burn-down rate
explanation: In a flow-based (kanban) approach, you multiply the cycle time (average time for each user story to be completed) by the throughput (how many user stories are being completed per unit time, such as per week or month) to forecast how much work can be completed in three months. Multiplying velocity by remaining user stories is a scrum-specific approach. Story points and sprint burn-down rates are also scrum-specific metrics, not appropriate for a flow-based kanban process.

question: Your product owner wants to bring the next feature forward so that it is done in parallel to the current feature. You make the necessary adjustments to the product backlog and decide to adjust the product roadmap which you have visualized as a Gantt chart. What will you do next to make this happen?
options: A. Use a schedule lag
B. Use a schedule lead
C. Change the feature to finish-to-start dependency
D. Change the feature to start-to-start dependency
explanation: Schedule lead is used to bring an item forward in time. Using the precedence diagramming method with a finish-to-start relationship, you can apply a lead so the second item can start before the first item finishes, allowing parallel work while maintaining delivery order. Schedule lag moves items backward, not forward. While finish-to-start is the current dependency, a lead applied to it achieves the goal, making schedule lead the best answer.

question: You are working on a project where you have been completing the deliverables in sequence. The project work is starting to fall behind. The project sponsor asks you to speed up the work and reduce the time for the project to be delivered but also makes it very clear that there is no more money to use when speeding up the work. What will you do next?
options: A. Use schedule crashing to add resources
B. Use schedule fast tracking to perform activities in parallel
C. Use resource leveling to balance the schedule
D. Negotiate with the sponsor for additional budget
explanation: Fast tracking is the appropriate schedule compression technique when budget constraints exist. It involves performing activities that are normally done in sequence in parallel instead. Schedule crashing requires additional resources and budget, which are not available. Resource leveling actually makes schedules longer by sequencing parallel work. Fast tracking is the only option that compresses the schedule without requiring additional money.
topic: Schedule Compression - Fast Tracking vs Crashing

question: After reviewing your project schedule, you notice you are behind by 3 weeks and the schedule needs to be compressed. Your project does not have enough budget to crash the schedule. You check with the lead for each area and review the schedule as to whether they can fast track their deliverables. Which dependencies should you focus on when fast tracking?
options: A. Discretionary dependencies
B. Mandatory dependencies
C. External dependencies
D. Industry-regulated dependencies
explanation: Discretionary dependencies are based on project preferences and are the most easily modifiable. They are not set in stone and can be adjusted for fast tracking. Mandatory dependencies are fixed requirements and cannot be modified. External dependencies are outside project control and difficult to change. Industry-regulated dependencies have strict regulatory requirements and cannot be modified. When compressing schedules through fast tracking, focus on discretionary dependencies as they offer the most flexibility.

question: You are transitioning your project from waterfall to Agile. Your key customer asks you for a project schedule. The project team believes that using a project schedule is from the old waterfall way of working and they shouldn't have to create one. What will you do next?
options: A. Create a project Gantt chart to visualize the activities within the project
B. Determine the project's planned versus actual value instead of working with a schedule directly
C. Advise the customer that we don't need to plan ahead in an adaptive way of work
D. Ask your team to develop a high-level release plan like a product road map showing the features to be included in each release
explanation: In adaptive/Agile approaches, a high-level release plan (product road map) shows basic features and functionality to be included in each release using incremental planning. This addresses the customer's need for scheduling while aligning with Agile principles. While Gantt charts can work, a product road map is more Agile-centric. Planned versus actual value focuses on variance analysis rather than scheduling. Advising against planning ahead is incorrect—product road maps are essential for showing upcoming features and ensuring value delivery.

question: You are working in a project team that has recently moved to an Agile way of work. Your business analysts want to gather all requirements, match solutions to scope, and create a work breakdown structure for the whole deliverable. Your product owner says this will take too long and is not Agile enough. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to review the scope management plan for the proper process
B. Break the deliverable down into smaller pieces so you can deliver those faster
C. Ask your team to time box work on the highest priority items in the backlog, update the estimates, and then reprioritize them as necessary
D. Review the project charter for the high-level scope and then use this to begin delivery quickly
explanation: Time boxing work on highest-priority backlog items and reprioritizing as necessary is the most Agile-centric approach. It incorporates key Agile practices: prioritization, time boxing, estimate updates, and continuous reprioritization. Breaking deliverables into smaller pieces (B) may not create usable increments. Reviewing the scope management plan (A) and project charter (D) do not address the Agile concern about spending too much time upfront on complete analysis. Adaptive approaches emphasize time boxes and prioritized backlogs where teams self-manage their work.

question: While reviewing current project progress with your project sponsor, they notice that the system solution is missing a critical piece that will impact the benefit of the project. The project sponsor approves this scope addition. Your project has a planned value of $520,000 and an actual cost of $535,000. What will you do next to add this critical piece of scope?
options: A. Work with the PMO to unlock the project management reserves for the extra work
B. Ask the product owner to reprioritize the backlog and see if the new work will fit
C. Perform the work within your normal budget as your project is on track
D. Raise a change request for the changes and gain approval from the Change Control Board
explanation: Management reserves are specifically set aside for unexpected activities related to in-scope work. Since the project sponsor has already approved this scope as necessary for benefit realization, it qualifies as in-scope work that should be funded through management reserves, which may be managed by the PMO at program or portfolio levels. Option B assumes an Agile project and would displace other work. Option C is incorrect because the project is over budget ($535,000 actual vs. $520,000 planned). Option D is unnecessary since the project sponsor (typically part of the Change Control Board) has already approved it.

question: You are in the planning stages of an Agile project where functional managers and senior users have provided high-level requirements and scope. You have been asked to put together a project team that will be able to deliver quickly. What will you do next?
options: A. Select project team members from each city to ensure diversity of knowledge within the team
B. Put together a resource management plan outlining the resources
C. Focus on team collocation and transparency in the work environment to enable fast delivery
D. Assign team members based on their availability and skill sets
explanation: In Agile projects, high-performing teams benefit from collocation and transparency, which enable learning by osmosis, close collaboration, and faster communication—critical for quick delivery. While diversity of knowledge (A) has merit, collocation is specifically emphasized in Agile as enabling rapid delivery. A detailed resource management plan (B) is more aligned with traditional project management and may slow down the startup. Assigning by availability alone (D) does not ensure team capability for rapid delivery. Agile emphasizes team environment and working relationships over formal resource planning documents.

question: When planning for the team, the project sponsor considers the ability and necessity for team members to work in the same location. What is a key advantage of small project teams that can work in the same room?
options: A. It reduces the need for a project manager
B. It allows the team to take advantage of osmotic communication and solve problems as they arise
C. It eliminates the need for formal communication plans
D. It guarantees project success on the first delivery
explanation: Small teams working in the same location can leverage osmotic communication—overhearing conversations and solving problems together quickly. This reduces communication channels (which increase exponentially with team size) and enables rapid problem-solving. This relates to team composition and structure principles in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You are putting together a project team to deliver an accounting system to officers around the country. The accounting system has not been used by anyone in your organization before. The project is quite high risk and the delivery needs to be right the first time. What will you do next?
options: A. Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve problems as they arise
B. Use a predictive project approach and source part of your team externally to access skill sets in the new system
C. Train existing internal staff in the new system and have them work on the project
D. Perform a make or buy analysis on the different software options
explanation: Since the system is new to the organization and the project is high risk with a single delivery requirement, a predictive (waterfall) approach is appropriate. Sourcing part of the team externally brings specialized skills with the new system, which is critical. Training internal staff might work but would introduce team formation delays and lack of expertise in the new system. The make or buy analysis is past the decision point.
topic: Team Composition and Structure, Resource Planning, Project Approach Selection

question: You are halfway through your agile project when a significant change to the project scope is discovered after finding that the project would not be able to deliver the promised benefits. You check your project budget and find that contingency and management reserves are available. What will you do next?
options: A. Take the project scope change to the Change Control Board for approval
B. Use the available contingency reserves to make the appropriate change to the project scope
C. Use the available management reserves to make the appropriate change to the project scope
D. Request additional budget from the project sponsor before proceeding
explanation: Any significant scope change must go through the formal Change Control Board for approval, regardless of available reserves. Contingency reserves are for identified risks, and management reserves are for unforeseen items, but both must be approved through the change control process. Using reserves without proper change control and approval bypasses necessary governance and accountability procedures.
topic: Change Control, Scope Management, Reserve Management

question: You are working on an agile project as a Scrum Master where the functional manager has worked with some people in your development team before. The functional manager from the business approaches them directly to see if they can add a feature to the product in their current Sprint. What will you do next?
options: A. Adjust the Sprint backlog to allow for the additional feature
B. Adjust the product backlog to add the customer requirement
C. Ask the manager to speak with the product owner for product priorities, then protect the team from direct requests in the future
D. Ask the manager to create a change request so it can go through the proper process
explanation: In an agile team, the product owner is the single point of decision for prioritizing work and ensuring the highest value work is being done. The functional manager should present the request to the product owner, not directly to the team. As Scrum Master, you have a responsibility to protect the team from external distractions and maintain focus on the current Sprint. This is called being a diversion shield, which prevents the team's productivity from being compromised by outside requests.
topic: Scrum Master Role and Team Protection in Agile

question: You are working as a product owner and have placed high-level information about the product in the product backlog. The highest priority feature is at the top. Senior users informing the project are unable to clearly articulate their requirements and are concerned that the product will not truly meet their needs. What will you do next?
options: A. Proceed with the highest priority item during the first Sprint
B. Work with the customer to create mockups and prototypes to discover what works for them
C. Ask your stakeholders to submit a change request and update the project scope accordingly
D. Begin breaking down the work into user stories to place into the Sprint
explanation: When requirements are not clearly defined, prototypes, demonstrations, storyboards, and mockups can be used to evolve those requirements. This approach allows stakeholders to see what the product will look like and say 'I know it when I see it,' enabling them to provide feedback and adjustments before development begins. Proceeding with the Sprint (option A) or breaking down work into user stories (option D) would be premature without clear requirements. Submitting a change request (option C) is not the appropriate response for discovering initial requirements.

question: You are working in an agile team who are trying to break down the project requirements and scope. They have created a scope statement and a work breakdown structure. However, you are not sure how it will work now that they are using Sprints and user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Assign activities to each of the scope items in the work breakdown structure and place them on a Gantt chart
B. Create a product road map with the team using the lowest most detailed part of the work breakdown structure
C. Convert the work breakdown structure into a Sprint backlog for immediate use
D. Abandon the work breakdown structure and rely solely on user stories
explanation: In an agile environment, a work breakdown structure can still be valuable but should be converted into a product road map. Using the lowest, most detailed parts of the work breakdown structure to create a product road map allows the team to maintain structure while operating within an agile framework with Sprints and user stories. While a Gantt chart (option A) is traditional project management approach that can work in hybrid environments, creating a product road map is the more appropriate agile practice. The work breakdown structure should not be abandoned entirely (option D), and converting directly to a Sprint backlog (option C) may skip important planning steps.
topic: Work Breakdown Structure in Agile Environments

question: Your project team is trialling an agile way of work. The product owner has a clear vision for the product. There is confusion in the team as to how much work should be done on a user story before it's taken to the Sprint review and how they will know that a story is complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the user story acceptance criteria and your team's definition of done
B. Review the user story with multiple users to ensure it's correct
C. Ask the product owner to sign off on the completed user story
D. Ensure quality testing on the user story is completed and passed
explanation: The definition of done is the key artifact that clarifies when work on a user story is complete. Acceptance criteria and the definition of done together ensure the team knows what 'done' means. While the other options are good practices, they are all components that feed into the acceptance criteria and definition of done rather than the primary mechanism for establishing completion standards.
topic: Agile Ceremonies and Artifacts - Definition of Done

question: You're working in an agile team where executives in the team you're delivering to are asking for a large display of reports to be created every two weeks showing how the project is tracking and updating them on the cost and benefits. What will you do next?
options: A. Collect the planned value versus earned value to show the executives any variance in the plan
B. Collocate the team with the executives so they can answer any questions as they arise
C. Suggest a demonstration of the feature developed during each Sprint instead and a burndown chart of future product releases
D. Create the desired reports for the executives to keep them happy
explanation: In agile projects, the best approach is to demonstrate actual value and features delivered through Sprint reviews and use live information radiators instead of large reports. This focuses on demonstrating the real customer value and using actual working software rather than just creating reports for reporting's sake. While collocation is good for osmosis learning, demonstrating features with a burndown chart of future product releases is more agile-centric and better addresses the need to show progress and value.

question: You're working in an agile project team that has increased in size and complexity since the project began. In reviewing the throughput of work with your team you notice fewer and fewer user stories being completed, and the roadmap of features scheduled to go live is being pushed out. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Place a limit on the work in progress and work with the team to reduce the cycle time
B. Increase meetings with the team
C. Ask the team to work overtime until the number of features reduces
D. Put a pull system in place where the team can pull work only when they are ready
explanation: Limiting work in progress (WIP) helps prevent context switching and multitasking, which are the likely causes of decreased throughput in a larger, more complex team. By focusing on fewer concurrent tasks, the team can complete work faster and reduce cycle time. Option B (more meetings) reduces productivity. Option C (overtime) is anti-agile and unsustainable, leading to burnout. Option D (pull system without WIP limits) could lead to too much work in progress if team members get blocked and pull additional work rather than focusing on unblocking current work.
topic: Agile Team Capacity and Work In Progress Management

question: Your agile project team has only recently formed, the product is still unclear, and there have been disagreements and minor conflicts over the past two weeks as the team finds their rhythm. You would like things to improve but you cannot quite pinpoint or address the problem. What will you do next?
options: A. Have the team complete a net promoter score with comments
B. Have the team complete a net promoter score with comments and ask for improvement ideas at the next retrospective
C. Hold a workshop with your team and ask them why they are unhappy
D. Have the team pair up together so they can learn from each other
explanation: The best approach is to use a net promoter score to measure stakeholder satisfaction, gather comments, and then use the retrospective to capture improvement ideas and address root causes. While pairing is an agile practice, it may not address the underlying issues. Workshops focused on complaints without a structured improvement process can be counterproductive. A net promoter score alone without follow-up retrospective action is incomplete.

question: Your project has faced serious impacts in obtaining materials due to supply chain disruptions from a global pandemic and is currently 3 weeks behind schedule. You take this information to the next project steering committee meeting where they advise they need more information on how much the project will end up costing. What will you do next?
options: A. Use the current budget with no deviations until it runs out
B. Use the project burndown chart to see the burn rate of accounting funds
C. Use regression analysis to see the current rate of spending and forecast the final cost
D. Use the project's estimate at completion (EAC)
explanation: Estimate at Completion (EAC) is the most appropriate forecast metric to show the steering committee the likely final project cost. EAC is calculated using the formula: Budget at Completion (BAC) ÷ Cost Performance Index (CPI), where CPI is earned value divided by actual cost. This reflects current performance trends. Regression analysis may work but EAC is the standard earned value forecasting technique. Burndown charts measure feature completion, not cost. Using the current budget with no adjustments does not address the variance caused by delays.
topic: Earned Value Management and Budget Forecasting

question: You are working on an agile project where leaders are spending a considerable amount of time in meetings to stay informed about progress, which reduces time spent on creating the actual product. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your project sponsor to wait until a steering committee meeting for the information
B. Suggest the leaders come to the Sprint review and gather information from the information radiator
C. Share your working project schedule and budget with the executives instead of creating reports
D. Demonstrate to the customer that you have visible charts and information radiators
explanation: Information radiators (Big Visible Charts) allow stakeholders to pull information anytime without formal meetings. The Sprint review demonstrates actual completed work. This approach is more live and transparent than sharing static reports, and it eliminates the need for frequent status meetings that add non-value-added work.

question: You are working on a project that has planned as much as they can up front, and you would like the team to move to a more agile way of working. Currently the work is hidden, the team catches up only once a week in a working group, and it is hard to see what each person is working on. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask your team to report on their work in a written status update each day so you can keep track
B. Have more working group meetings so you are always up to date on what the team is working on
C. Ask the team to move their work to a central visible task board like a Kanban board and have a short meeting each day to discuss progress on their cards like a standup meeting
D. Remove the team meetings altogether and trust your team to get the work done
explanation: A Kanban board with daily standups creates visibility and transparency while reducing non-value-added work. Standups are short (15 minutes) and focused on removing blockers and ensuring the team has what they need. This is better than daily written updates (which is non-value-added work), more frequent meetings, or removing communication entirely.
topic: Agile Ceremonies and Visual Controls (Kanban Board)

question: You are working on a hybrid project where the majority of the work has been planned up front, the team manages their work via a Kanban board and reports on progress and blockers each day. As the project evolves, stakeholders have added more and more scope to the product, with one executive raising an urgent feature that needs to be added. What will you do next?
options: A. Limit the number of cards in the ready or to-do column and ask the product owner to work with stakeholder requests, noting the need to swap the new item for an existing item
B. Match the number of user stories to the team's velocity to ensure a sustainable pace
C. Work with the stakeholder to raise a change request and note the outcome in a change log
D. Review the impact of the scope change with the stakeholder using the work breakdown structure dictionary
explanation: In a hybrid Kanban environment, work-in-progress limits and prioritization control scope creep. The product owner must help stakeholders understand that adding new features means deprioritizing existing items. This maintains sustainable pace and ensures the team works on the highest-value items. A change log is less appropriate for agile scope management, and the WBS dictionary is not used for impact reviews.

question: You are working on a project with a vendor where there is significant ambiguity involved. Many outcomes are possible with varying degrees of impact. What should you do to improve the situation?
options: A. Use experiments and prototypes and progressive elaboration to work your way through it.
B. Ask your team for additional reporting around different options so you can make an informed decision.
C. Add all the different options into your project scope so all your bases are covered.
D. Work more closely with your senior users so they can inform you of the product requirements.
explanation: The correct answer uses experiments, prototypes, and progressive elaboration to navigate ambiguity by iteratively learning which options are viable. This approach is ideal when there are multiple possible outcomes—you test them in small increments, gather feedback, and adjust accordingly rather than trying to decide everything upfront. Adding all options to scope (C) doesn't resolve the ambiguity; it merely adds complexity. Additional reporting (B) and closer user engagement (D), while potentially helpful, do not directly address the fundamental ambiguity or provide a mechanism to work through multiple possible outcomes effectively.

question: You are working on a project that integrates with many other systems within your organization. The system is quite complex, and you cannot decide which features to deliver first. How will you deal with the complexity?
options: A. List out the possible features you can deliver and prioritize them by cost over benefit
B. Simulate decoupling connecting parts to simplify the number of variables
C. Reframe your view by brainstorming with a diverse set of stakeholders
D. Provide an incentive for your project team for whoever can solve this complex problem
explanation: Methods for dealing with complexity include systems-based approaches such as decoupling and simulation. This allows you to test scenarios without physically implementing changes. While brainstorming with diverse stakeholders and prioritization are good practices, simulation of decoupling is the most direct technical approach to reducing complexity. Providing incentives alone does not address the systemic complexity. Reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 121, Complexity section.

question: You are working on a project that requires a very particular programming skill set that is not very common. The job market is the hottest the industry has seen in two decades, people are hard to come by and leave often, and the programming language is evolving at a quick pace. What will you do next?
options: A. Develop the product from scratch in a more common programming language
B. Ask your whole team to learn the programming skill set to cover the risk
C. Use alternatives analysis to gather different options and ensure a sufficient cost reserve to help complete your project
D. Raise a risk to your project completion with the threat of project team member skill sets
explanation: This scenario describes volatility, an environment subject to rapid and unpredictable change, including ongoing fluctuations in available skill sets. The best response to volatility is to use alternatives analysis to explore different options and establish cost reserves as contingencies. While raising the risk is appropriate, alternatives analysis combined with cost reserves represents a more mature and pragmatic approach to dealing with volatility. Reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Edition, page 122, Volatility section.
topic: Volatility Management and Risk Response Planning

question: Your project management office has no prescribed way of work so they're working with a vendor who supplies an extreme programming framework at a significant cost. However, projects are still running behind schedule, over budget, and with low customer satisfaction. What should you do next?
options: A. Tailor the approach to suit the organization operating environment and project needs
B. Use a waterfall approach instead to ensure the proper planning
C. Switch to scrum instead as it's a more popular framework
D. Tighten the controls around the project environment with a more directive PMO approach
explanation: Tailoring is performed to better suit the organization operating environment and project needs. Projects operate in complex environments that need to balance competing demands. Simply switching frameworks or tightening controls without addressing the underlying fit between the framework and the organization's context will not solve the problem.

question: You're a manager of a PMO in a large organization which has recently undertaken a companywide way of working transformation. You need to select an initial development approach for your project which you will then tailor to suit your organization and project needs. What will you do next?
options: A. Use a framework that best engages your executive team through easy to use graphics and presentations
B. Select the most popular current framework to keep up with what other organizations are doing
C. Select multiple development approaches as a baseline for your organization
D. Use a suitability filter to assess the most appropriate development approach
explanation: A suitability filter helps project teams consider whether a project has characteristics that lend themselves towards a predictive, hybrid, or adaptive approach. This allows you to select one as a base and then enable the ability to tailor to your project or organization's circumstances. Relying on graphics, popularity, or multiple frameworks without a structured assessment will not result in the best fit for your organization.

question: Your organization is starting to move to an agile way of work and they're setting up a value delivery office (VDO) to complement the existing PMO. The executives in the area have begun working through what the function looks like and the roles and responsibilities that might exist. They approach you for help. What will you tell them about the VDO's primary focus?
options: A. The VDO is responsible for the value delivered and owns the programs within the organization
B. The VDO focuses on coaching, building adaptive skills, growing team capabilities, and mentoring sponsors and product owners
C. The VDO is responsible for allocating agile resources such as scrum masters and product owners effectively to project teams
D. The VDO creates the organizational framework to be used on each project throughout the company
explanation: In an agile environment, a value delivery office focuses on coaching, facilitation, and mentoring rather than directing. It builds adaptive skills, grows team capabilities, and mentors sponsors and product owners to be more effective. This coaching and enabling approach is distinct from the more directive PMO functions described in the other options.
topic: Agile Organizational Roles and Value Delivery

question: You have been planning a project for the last 2 months, gathering requirements, scope, and assigning tasks to a schedule. As you get closer to the execution phase of your project, you decide what delivery approach you will use. The requirements are likely to change, require a medium amount of compliance, but can be released in one go. What delivery approach will you use?
options: A. Predictive
B. Incremental
C. Agile or adaptive
D. Iterative
explanation: An iterative delivery approach involves gathering feedback and improving over time while delivering in one release. Incremental releases deliver increments of value like features over time. Predictive plans everything up front and then delivers in one go. Agile gathers feedback and releases incrementally. This scenario describes iterating towards success with a single release, which is the defining characteristic of an iterative approach.

question: Your agile team is experiencing too much work in progress and high rates of scrap. What is the best recommendation to address this issue?
options: A. Add more people to the project to complete the work faster
B. Use value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work and identify issues
C. Ask your team to work overtime to cover the extra work
D. Review the scope of the project and reduce items with low customer impact
explanation: When there is too much work in progress or high rates of scrap, the recommended approach is to use techniques like value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualize the work, identify the issues, and propose solutions. This allows teams to limit work in progress and reduce context switching. Adding more people can complicate communication and processes. Asking for overtime is unsustainable and leads to disengagement. While scope review is a decent practice, it is not the best answer for addressing work-in-progress issues. This guidance is found in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 151 under common situations and tailoring suggestions.
topic: Agile Work Management and Process Improvement

question: Your product owner has prioritized the product backlog and the team has created user stories with estimates filling a two-week Sprint. However, you notice that pieces being completed are being rejected by the customer due to defects and missed requirements. What should you do next?
options: A. Create the project scope from scratch with the project team and customers
B. Have an iteration zero planning Sprint with your project team to set it up for success
C. Add more feedback loops such as shoulder checks, peer review, unit testing, and system testing
D. Have less frequent customer demonstrations so they only see the product when it is correct
explanation: When a product is facing poor quality deliverables, the recommended tailoring suggestion is to add more feedback loops and quality assurance steps. These include demonstrations and reviews, peer review of items, shoulder checks, pair programming, quality audits, and various levels of testing such as unit testing, system testing, user acceptance testing, regression testing, and automated testing. By catching issues earlier through multiple feedback loops, especially at the requirements stage, costs are minimized. Creating scope from scratch is excessive, iteration zero delays value delivery, and less frequent demonstrations reduce customer feedback opportunities. This guidance is found in the PMBOK Guide, 7th Edition, page 151.
topic: Quality Assurance and Feedback Loops in Agile

question: Your project team has started to find their rhythm, but you notice they seem disengaged and are dragging their feet on normal tasks. You check the budget and confirm the team is paid fairly for the market and within the same range for the role. What is the best way to improve engagement?
options: A. Look for ways to reward team members with bonuses above their normal pay rate
B. Move team members around to different roles frequently so they experience job variety
C. Give the team a paid day off
D. Establish ways for the team to grow and advance
explanation: According to Herzberg's motivational theory, once hygiene factors such as fair compensation and physical environment are satisfied, people are motivated by factors such as achievement, growth, and advancement. While bonuses, role variety, and paid time off may provide temporary relief, they wear off quickly. The most effective long-term approach is to establish career paths, learning pathways, and opportunities for growth and advancement that give team members a sense of progress. This taps into the intrinsic motivational factors that sustain engagement beyond basic compensation.
topic: Team Motivation and Herzberg's Motivational Theory

question: You are working as a project manager on a regulatory change for your organization. You notice that the team is not communicating as well as they could be and you put together a plan to improve team communication by increasing its effectiveness and richness. What will you do?
options: A. Include methods that will handle multiple information cues such as body language, voice, and words
B. Facilitate rapid feedback to ensure everyone is on the same page
C. Utilize natural language avoiding jargon or other complicated language
D. Include ways to get sign-off in writing so it can be recorded and reviewed later when needed
explanation: According to Alistair Cockburn's Crystal method and media richness theory, effectiveness and richness of communication is achieved through methods that handle multiple information cues (body language, voice, words), facilitate rapid feedback, establish personal focus, and utilize natural language. Option A directly addresses the core principle of media richness by including methods to handle multiple information cues, which is the foundation of effective communication channels.

question: You have been planning a project to deliver a process change for the past 4 weeks. The requirements and scope have been signed off by the business owner and the team are moving into delivery and execution. The work begins in line with the project plan and scope. What will you do next?
options: A. Monitor and control the project work
B. Execute the project work
C. Plan the work
D. Formally sign off the project
explanation: The project has moved into the executing phase. As project work is executed, it must then be monitored and controlled to ensure it stays on track and meets its goals. The project management process groups are initiating, planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, and closing. These may be performed in sequence or iterated, but once execution begins, monitoring and controlling is the concurrent activity. This is from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition page 170 under process groups.

question: You are working as a product owner. While your sales and revenue have been increasing over the past 8 months, the overall profit has decreased by 17%. Your executive manager asks you to address the situation. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Stop the current work and set a planning session with your team to address the situation
B. Ask your team to work overtime on the current features in your backlog to get the work done
C. Perform benchmarking with a similar yet non-competitive company and add any process improvements to your product backlog
D. Look for opportunities to partner with other projects and products in your organization and increase your sales further
explanation: The core issue is that profit is decreasing while sales are increasing, which means costs are too high. Performing benchmarking to find gaps in your process and then executing improvements with your team will help reduce costs and improve profitability. Option D would worsen the problem by increasing sales without addressing the root cause of rising costs. Options A and B would be disruptive and unsustainable. As a product owner, your responsibility includes increasing profit. This is from the PMBOK Guide 7th Edition page 175 under data gathering and analysis and benchmarking.

question: Your team is working on estimating user stories for an upcoming sprint. Which approach would be most effective for helping the team perform relative estimating?
options: A. Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the acceptance criteria
B. Estimate the effort for the overall feature and reduce the need for individual story estimates
C. Have the team size stories in points relative to the smallest piece, often using a Fibonacci scale, with input from all team members including testers and developers
D. Base the estimates on cost per hour and add them up after the work is done to ensure accuracy
explanation: Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories where they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece, often in story points and frequently using a Fibonacci scale. This approach benefits from collaborative input from all team members involved in the work (developers, testers, etc.), which is a key advantage over individual estimating. The other options either miss the collaborative aspect, don't address the need for story-level estimates for sprint velocity calculation, or confuse estimating with actual cost tracking.

question: You are halfway through your current sprint of two weeks. The work is progressing well, however you notice that there is not enough work ready and elaborated for when the next sprint begins. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the work that could be accomplished
B. Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily standup and ask your team to swarm around the problem
C. Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the team
D. Place the cards on the kanban board so the team can see the work and get started
explanation: A backlog refinement meeting is the appropriate action to take during the current sprint to progressively elaborate and prioritize the backlog for the upcoming iteration. The team needs to identify work that meets the definition of ready before the next sprint begins. A retrospective occurs at the end of a sprint, making it too late to prepare for the upcoming sprint. The daily standup is not the appropriate forum for this type of planning, and work must be ready according to the definition of ready before being placed on a board.

question: You have completed a make-or-buy analysis in your project and found that part of your deliverable is best created by an external vendor. You work with your project team to create the procurement strategy, source selection criteria, and statement of work. What should you do next?
options: A. Gather the seller proposals including technical details and price
B. Meet with prospective sellers to ensure all vendors have a common understanding of the procurement
C. Perform source selection analysis on what you want from prospective vendors
D. Create the procurement agreement for the third-party vendor to provide their specified services
explanation: A bidder conference (also called a contractor conference, vendor conference, or pre-bid conference) is an output of plan procurement management where you meet with prospective sellers prior to the preparation of a bid or proposal to ensure all prospective bidders have a clear and common understanding of the procurement. This meeting comes after you have established your procurement strategy and source selection criteria, but before you gather actual proposals. Source selection analysis occurs later, and the procurement agreement is created only after a seller has been selected.

question: Your agile project has recently gained some new stakeholders after a department restructure. They complain to you that they are not seeing enough traction of work delivered through the project and they are worried that it will not meet their requirements. What should you do next?
options: A. Invite them to the daily standup so they can see the work that happens on a daily basis
B. Invite them to iteration planning so they can choose what the team works on
C. Schedule a backlog refinement session to understand their specific requirements
D. Provide them with a detailed project schedule and budget forecast
explanation: Inviting new stakeholders to iteration planning (sprint planning) allows them to understand what work will be done in the upcoming sprint and ensures their requirements and priorities are being considered. This helps address their concerns about traction and requirements being met. While the daily standup can be open to stakeholders in some methodologies, iteration planning is the more appropriate venue for stakeholder involvement in prioritization decisions. The daily standup's primary purpose is for the development team to coordinate work, whereas iteration planning is where priorities and requirements are discussed. Backlog refinement may be helpful, but iteration planning more directly addresses their concerns about work selection and priority.

question: You are an agile project manager holding daily standups with your team. You notice people accusing other team members of not doing their job and there are some tasks that are not mentioned anywhere and no one is responsible for. The team velocity has slowed in the last three Sprints. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the team charter to clarify team roles and agreed ways of managing conflict
B. Raise it as an issue in the next retrospective and come to a solution with the team
C. Assign team roles to those you think are best suited
D. Work with your team to size user stories correctly so velocity improves
explanation: Since tasks are not mentioned anywhere and roles are not defined in the team charter, referring to the charter would not help. The agile approach is to problem-solve with the team in a retrospective—a regularly occurring workshop where the team explores their work to improve their process and product. This allows the team to collaboratively identify missing tasks, clarify responsibilities, and address conflicts. While assigning roles unilaterally might seem efficient, agile emphasizes team collaboration and self-organization.

question: A product owner prioritizes features to improve usability and profitability of a product. Which measure would best help the product owner understand customer satisfaction and loyalty?
options: A. Net promoter score
B. Cost versus benefit analysis
C. Average app availability and uptime
D. Lowest cost with highest potential benefit
explanation: Net promoter score (NPS) is a customer-focused measure that gauges the willingness of customers to recommend a product or service to others, directly measuring overall customer satisfaction and loyalty. While cost-benefit analysis, app availability, and cost-benefit prioritization are useful project management tools, they do not specifically measure customer satisfaction and loyalty from a product owner's perspective.
topic: Product Owner Responsibilities and Agile Metrics

question: Your project team have planned the requirements, scope, schedule, and cost for constructing a new building in your organization. An executive from another area approaches you as your project is funded and about to begin execution and asks if you can help them deliver a website as part of your work. What will you do next?
options: A. Raise the request as an issue in your project's issue log
B. Add the website to your project scope and adjust your project scope, schedule, and cost
C. Raise a change request for the added scope and review the impact to your project schedule and cost
D. Offer to help them create a business case for the new idea and get funding for a new project
explanation: Building a website is fundamentally different from constructing a physical building and requires different resources, expertise, and potentially different funding. This represents a separate project that needs its own business case, project charter, and funding rather than being added to an existing construction project. A business case provides a value proposition and financial analysis necessary for formal project approval and kickoff.
topic: Scope Management and Business Case Development

question: You are working on an agile project and have gathered feature ideas from various parts of your organization including frontline staff, executives, and direct customer research. You have many feature ideas to work with. What should you do next?
options: A. Break down each feature into epics and user stories, then size the work with your team to determine effort level
B. Add all items into your product backlog and work on the ones requested by managers first
C. Work with the requesters and your technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature
D. Perform precedence diagramming on each feature to determine their dependencies
explanation: Working with requesters and technical experts to create a business model canvas for each feature provides a one-page visual summary describing the value proposition, infrastructure, customers, and finances. This gives the information needed to prioritize effectively based on benefit and cost. While breaking down into epics and stories could work, it's premature before prioritization. Adding all items and working on manager-requested items first ignores the need to prioritize by benefit and cost. Precedence diagramming requires more detailed information about the solution and dependencies, which you don't have at this stage.

question: You are integrating a third-party system as part of your project. The developers on your project team have not worked on this system before but believe the language and layout are the same and should be able to implement it. Adding time for the uncertainty, they give a high-level estimate of 5 weeks. What will you do next?
options: A. Note the estimate of 5 weeks and update the assumption log with the technical assumption that the languages and layout are the same
B. Break down the integration into user stories and use a bottom-up approach to estimate
C. Check the estimate using parametric estimating to increase its accuracy
D. Gather your whole team and customers together and estimate using wideband Delphi or planning poker
explanation: Recording the estimate and documenting the underlying technical assumption in the assumption log is the appropriate next step when estimates are based on assumptions. This captures the assumption so that if it proves false, you can adjust the estimate. The assumption log is critical for tracking factors considered to be true without proof. While user story breakdown, parametric estimating, and wideband Delphi are all valid estimation techniques, they are not the priority here since you already have a reasonable estimate and need to capture the assumption underlying it first.

question: You are working on an agile project where your senior developer raises a concern during the daily standup that the system doesn't seem to be able to connect to some parts of the organization after a feature upgrade release. What should you do next?
options: A. Replan the project to ensure the correct scope is worked on that will connect to the organization
B. Assign it to a responsible party and work with them to find a resolution
C. Raise the concern as a risk and brainstorm risk responses
D. Escalate the issue to the technical managers so they can resolve it
explanation: This is an active issue that has already occurred and is impacting the project. The correct process is to raise it in the issue log, assign it to a responsible party, and work with them to find a resolution. Option C is incorrect because the problem has already happened, making it an issue rather than a risk. Option D is too narrow—while technical managers may be involved, the issue should be assigned to the responsible party who may not necessarily be a technical manager.

question: You are working on a hybrid project to replace an aging customer system. A change request has been approved by the Change Control Board for a missing key requirement found by a senior user that was not included in the baselined project scope. What should you do next?
options: A. Place the new requirement into the Sprint backlog for the next Sprint
B. Elaborate on the requirement and turn it into user stories and acceptance criteria
C. Add the missing requirement to the issue log and problem solve with your team to ensure it does not happen again
D. Note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to your stakeholders
explanation: After the Change Control Board has approved the change request, the first step in the Change Control process is to note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to affected stakeholders. Only after this communication should you elaborate on the requirement, turn it into user stories, place it in the Sprint backlog, and later problem solve during the retrospective to prevent similar issues in the future.

question: You are working on an agile project where your project team has raised multiple risks to project delivery, some of which may impact your cost and schedule significantly. Your program manager asks you to manage them using a risk adjusted backlog. What will you do next?
options: A. Adjust your risk register to include the work not completed if the risks are triggered
B. Adjust your backlog velocity so that your end date is not impacted by the raised risks
C. Gather risk mitigation responses from your retrospectives and assign them to an owner
D. Place at least one risk response into each Sprint in your backlog so they are managed and closed
explanation: A risk adjusted backlog includes both regular work and actions to address threats and opportunities. The correct approach is to place risk responses into each Sprint as backlog items so they are actively worked on and managed. This ensures that risk mitigation becomes part of the iterative delivery process rather than being handled separately.

question: A stakeholder approaches you and says they have not been engaged sufficiently even though they believe their team is highly impacted by the change. They advise they may block the project as a result. What should you do next?
options: A. Perform stakeholder engagement assessment with your stakeholder
B. Add them to the stakeholder register including their salary and interest information
C. Add them to the stakeholder register including their impact and influence information
D. Raise a risk to the project delivery due to the nature of their actions
explanation: The stakeholder register should record information about project stakeholders including their assessment and classification, impact, and influence. This ensures their details are properly documented and classified before taking further action. While a stakeholder engagement assessment is important, adding the stakeholder to the register with their impact and influence information is the more complete and appropriate next step. Salary information is not relevant to stakeholder management, and raising a risk without first attempting to manage the stakeholder is premature.

question: You are a product owner on an agile project and have created a product backlog of features in prioritized order for delivery. You ask the team to break down the first feature into user stories and estimate their effort. What will you do next?
options: A. Create an iteration plan with your team
B. Create the product roadmap with your team
C. Perform a Sprint review
D. Begin daily stand-ups with your team
explanation: After user stories are created and estimated with story points, the next step is to create an iteration plan (Sprint plan) with the team. This involves taking the estimated user stories and placing as much work as possible into the upcoming Sprint to match the team's velocity. The product roadmap would have been created earlier before breaking down into user stories. Sprint reviews occur after work is completed, and daily stand-ups cannot begin until the Sprint is planned.

question: You have planned and begun the current sprint where the project team has committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You need to see how the team is tracking towards their sprint goal. What will you do next?
options: A. Show them the kanban board
B. Review the team velocity chart
C. Review the sprint burndown chart
D. Perform a sprint review with the team
explanation: A sprint burndown chart is a graphical representation of work remaining in a sprint. It compares the ideal trend line with items actually completed, allowing you to see how the team is tracking towards completing all sprint goal items by the end of the sprint. While a kanban board shows cards by phase for a sprint, a velocity chart measures average velocity across sprints rather than real-time progress. A sprint review demonstrates completed work but occurs at the end of the sprint, not during it.

question: The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to your website delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code. It's a bit messy code and hasn't been cleaned up. You believe the user stories are moving slower now as a result, but you can't be sure. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the cycle time chart
B. Shadow the developers to overview the work they are doing
C. View the product roadmap for proposed release dates
D. Review the points in the Sprint backlog versus the last three Sprints velocity
explanation: A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of work items completed over time and can be displayed as a scatter diagram or bar chart. This directly measures how long individual user stories take to complete, allowing you to see if stories are indeed moving slower. Lead time measures from initial backlog entry to delivery, while velocity measures team capacity across entire Sprints rather than individual story performance. Shadowing developers and viewing the product roadmap do not provide quantitative metrics to confirm whether stories are moving slower.
topic: Agile Metrics and Artifacts (Cycle Time Chart)

question: You are working on an agile project with a full product roadmap for the next 6 months. Your project team is not sure what the next feature does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Review the scope baseline
B. Perform story mapping with your team
C. Perform sprint planning with your team
D. Perform a retrospective with your team
explanation: Story mapping is a visual model that breaks down features into user stories based on functionality, giving the team a holistic view of what they are building and why. It directly addresses the need to understand what a feature does and how to decompose it into deliverable user stories. The scope baseline is not typically used on agile projects. Sprint planning requires user stories to already exist. Retrospectives occur after Sprints to address what went well and what did not, but they do not help decompose upcoming features into user stories.

question: You are working with a vendor on an agile project. Multiple features are on the project's road map and may change and be reprioritized over time, making the scope of your project uncertain. How should you manage this vendor relationship in an agile project?
options: A. Ask your product owner to fix the features in scope so the project is more certain
B. Create a Cost Plus incentive fee contract with your procurement team
C. Create a firm fixed price contract with your procurement team
D. Use the external vendor funding to create a project team in house and complete the project
explanation: Cost Plus incentive fee contracts are appropriate when scope is uncertain or subject to frequent change. The vendor is reimbursed for actual costs incurred plus a fee, and the incentive fee motivates performance on factors like delivery speed. A firm fixed price contract would be unfair to both parties when scope is changing. Bringing the vendor in house introduces unnecessary complexity.

question: You have completed a make or buy analysis and agreed to hire a third-party vendor to complete part of your product. You have created the statement of work and have a list of acceptable sellers that meet your source selection criteria. The work is not complex and the sellers understand the scope. What is the best approach to obtain pricing from these sellers?
options: A. Send a request for proposal to all sellers
B. Send a request for information to the sellers
C. Send a request for quotation to the sellers
D. Hold a bid's conference to ensure all sellers are on the same page
explanation: A request for quotation is appropriate when requirements are clear, the work is straightforward, and you primarily need pricing information. A request for proposal is used for complex work requiring detailed proposals. A request for information is used when you need clarification. A bid's conference is unnecessary since you already have acceptable sellers with clear requirements.

question: You have created a high level view of the features your project will deliver and prioritized them into a prioritized product backlog. The development team is ready to start work but does not know where to begin because the information is too high level. These features need to be broken down. What should you do?
options: A. Ask the team to begin with the highest priority item first
B. Decompose the features into user stories for placement in a Sprint
C. Hold a retrospective with the team to understand what is blocking them
D. Reprioritize the features into an order that makes sense for the developers
explanation: User stories are brief descriptions of outcomes for specific users with enough information for team members to work on the item. Breaking down high-level features into user stories provides the detailed, actionable work items needed for Sprint planning and execution. A retrospective is held after a Sprint has started. Reprioritizing does not solve the granularity problem. Simply starting without decomposition does not address the lack of detail.
topic: Agile - Product Backlog and User Stories
